title: How to Use Intuition When Dealing with Fear
meta_description: Learn to read fear as a signal not a stop sign. Turn intuition into your guide when fear hits hard. Direct honest advice for empaths.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Dealing with Fear
og_description: Learn to read fear as a signal not a stop sign. Turn intuition into your guide when fear hits hard. Direct honest advice for empaths.
body:
Fear Isn't Your Enemy - It's Your Signal

Let's get one thing straight right now: fear doesn't mean you're wrong. It doesn't mean you're broken. And it sure as hell doesn't mean you should stop.

You've been told your whole life that fear is a stop sign. That when you feel scared, you should pull back, play it safe, stay small. But here's what nobody told you - fear is actually one of the most powerful signals your intuition can send. The trick is learning how to read it instead of just reacting to it.

I'm Kalesh, and I've spent years working with people who feel everything too much. People whose nervous systems are wired for radar instead of just reception. You're one of them. You feel things before they happen. You walk into a room and know the mood before anyone speaks. And when fear shows up - it hits you like a freight train.

But here's the thing I need you to hear: you can use that. Not just survive it - use it. Right?!

Why Your Fear and Your Intuition Are the Same Voice

Most people think intuition is this soft, gentle whisper. A kind inner guide that only speaks in peace and calm. And sure, sometimes it does. But more often than not, when you're dealing with real life - big decisions, hard changes, scary leaps - your intuition comes through as fear first.

Why? Because your intuition knows you're about to step into something that matters. Something that could change things. And your ego - the part of you that wants to keep you safe - picks up on that and sounds the alarm.

So you get that knot in your stomach. Your chest tightens. Your mind starts spinning worst-case scenarios. You think, "This must be a bad sign. I should back off."

But what if that knot isn't a warning? What if it's a recognition?

"Fear is not the opposite of intuition. It is the gatekeeper of it. When you learn to walk through fear instead of away from it, you stop being a prisoner of your own knowing."

Does that land? Because this is the shift that changes everything. You stop treating fear as a verdict and start treating it as data. You ask: "What is this fear actually telling me?"

Sometimes it's telling you there's real danger - and you should listen. But more often, it's telling you there's something important on the other side of this discomfort. Something your intuition already knows you need.

The First Step: Stop Trying to Get Rid of Fear

Here's where most people get stuck. They think the goal is to feel less fear. They buy books, do meditations, try affirmations - all in an effort to make the fear go away.

But that's like trying to make your smoke alarm stop by ignoring the smoke. The alarm isn't the problem. The alarm is telling you something. If you just rip the batteries out, you'll miss the fire.

So the first step isn't to calm down. It's to get curious.

When fear shows up, don't try to push it away. Don't tell yourself "there's nothing to be afraid of" - that's just bypassing. Instead, say something like: "Okay, I feel fear. What's this about? What's the message here?"

You might be surprised by what comes up. Maybe the fear is actually excitement in disguise - your body doesn't know the difference. Maybe it's a memory of a past hurt that's getting triggered by a present situation. Maybe it's your intuition saying, "Yes, this is the right move - and it's going to stretch you."

You won't know until you stop fighting it and start listening.

How to Tell the Difference: Fear vs. Intuition

This is the million-dollar question, right? How do you know if the fear is a warning or just noise?

Here's a simple approach I teach my students:

  • Fear that's a warning feels tight, constricting, and panicked. It comes with a sense of urgency that's sharp and immediate. It says "run" or "freeze" - and it's usually about something happening right now.
  • Fear that's intuition in disguise feels more like a deep, heavy knowing. It's uncomfortable but not frantic. There's a stillness underneath it. It says "this matters" - not "this is dangerous."

Think about it this way: fear that's a warning feels like a trap door opening beneath you. Fear that's intuition feels like standing at the edge of a cliff before you jump into water - terrifying, but also alive. You know you'll survive the jump. You might even love it. But your body is still screaming "don't!"

The key is to check in with your body. Where do you feel the fear? In your chest, throat, or head - that's usually ego fear. In your gut or your bones - that's usually intuitive fear. The gut doesn't lie. The head spins stories.

If you want to strengthen this discernment, I recommend The Gifts of Imperfection by Brené Brown (See on Amazon, paid link). Her work on vulnerability and fear is a masterclass in understanding the difference between fear that protects you and fear that holds you back.

The Practice: Using Fear as a Compass

Once you start seeing fear as a signal instead of a stop sign, you can actually use it to guide your decisions. Here's how:

1. Name the Fear Out Loud

Fear lives in the shadows. When you bring it into the light, it shrinks. Say it out loud: "I'm afraid that if I take this job, I'll fail." Or "I'm afraid that if I tell the truth, I'll lose this relationship."

Just naming it takes away some of its power. And it helps you see if the fear is based in reality or just a story your mind is telling you.

2. Ask: "What's the Worst That Could Happen?"

This is an old trick, but it works. Really sit with it. What's the actual worst-case scenario? And can you survive it? Usually, the answer is yes. You might not like it, but you'd survive.

Then ask: "What's the best that could happen?" Let yourself feel that possibility too. Your intuition already knows the answer - it's the one that makes your heart beat a little faster. Not with panic. With hope.

3. Take One Small Step Anyway

Action is the antidote to fear. But I'm not talking about jumping into the deep end. I'm talking about one small step. Something that moves you in the direction of what you're afraid of, but doesn't require you to commit fully.

If you're scared to start a new business, buy the domain name. If you're scared to have a hard conversation, write down what you want to say. If you're scared to move to a new city, book a weekend trip to check it out.

That small step sends a signal to your nervous system: "I'm doing this. I'm okay." And it lets your intuition speak more clearly because you've proven you can handle the discomfort.

4. Trust the Feeling That Comes After the Fear

Here's something most people miss: fear has a shelf life. It peaks, then it passes. And what's left after the fear passes is usually your intuition.

So when you're in the middle of a fear spiral, don't make a decision. Wait. Breathe. Let the wave pass. Then check in with yourself. What's the quiet feeling underneath? That's your answer.

For a deeper dive into how to work with fear as a spiritual practice, check out The Power of Now by Eckhart Tolle (See on Amazon, paid link). His work on presence is a powerful tool for getting underneath fear to the stillness where intuition lives.

What to Do When Fear Paralyzes You

Sometimes fear isn't just a signal - it's a full-body freeze. You can't think, can't move, can't breathe. This is when your nervous system has gone into survival mode. And in that state, you can't access your intuition at all.

If this is where you are, stop trying to "use" your intuition. You need to regulate your nervous system first.

Here's what works:

  • Ground yourself physically. Press your feet into the floor. Feel your back against the chair. Touch something solid - a wall, a table, a tree.
  • Breathe long and slow. Inhale for four counts, hold for four, exhale for six. This tells your body you're safe.
  • Move your body. Shake your hands, roll your shoulders, walk around. Fear gets stuck in the body - movement helps release it.
  • Name five things you can see. This pulls you out of the fear story and into the present moment.

Once your body calms down, your intuition will start to surface again. It was never gone - it was just drowned out by the alarm system. Give yourself permission to regulate before you try to decide anything.

Real-Life Example: Using Intuition Through Fear

I worked with a woman named Sarah who was terrified of leaving her corporate job. She knew - deep down - that she was meant to start her own coaching practice. But every time she thought about quitting, her fear went through the roof.

She'd wake up in the middle of the night with panic attacks. She'd obsess over worst-case scenarios: losing her savings, disappointing her family, failing publicly. She thought the fear meant she wasn't ready.

But when we sat down and looked at it together, we saw something different. The fear wasn't telling her not to leave. It was telling her that leaving mattered. That this was a big deal. That she was about to step into something that would change her life.

So she started small. She took one coaching client while still working her job. She saved up six months of expenses. She talked to her husband about the plan. Each small step reduced the fear a little. And underneath the fear, her intuition kept whispering: "This is right. Keep going."

She eventually left her job. She's been running her practice for three years now. And she still feels fear sometimes - but now she knows what it is. It's not a stop sign. It's a signal that she's on the right track.

When Fear Is Actually a Warning

I don't want to pretend that fear is always intuition in disguise. Sometimes it's a genuine warning. And you need to know the difference.

Fear that's a warning usually feels different. It's more like a gut punch than a knot. It comes with a sense of certainty - not "what if," but "no." It's not asking you to consider. It's telling you to stop.

If you're in a situation that feels unsafe - physically, emotionally, or energetically - listen to that fear. Don't override it because you think you should be brave. Bravery is knowing when to walk away too.

The difference? Warning fear feels clear and immediate. Intuitive fear feels heavy and uncertain. One says "danger" - the other says "growth."

If you want to learn more about how to trust your gut in high-stakes situations, Blink by Malcolm Gladwell (See on Amazon, paid link) is a fascinating look at how split-second intuition works - and when it's reliable versus when it's not.

The Bottom Line: Fear Is Part of the Path

You're never going to get rid of fear completely. And honestly, you shouldn't want to. Fear is part of being human. It's part of caring about things. It's part of taking risks and growing and changing.

The goal isn't to be fearless. The goal is to feel the fear and still trust yourself enough to move forward. To let fear be the smoke alarm that wakes you up - not the fire that burns you down.

Your intuition is already there. It's been there all along. It's the quiet voice underneath the noise. And when you learn to work with fear instead of against it, that voice gets louder. Clearer. More trustworthy.

So next time fear shows up, don't run from it. Don't fight it. Don't pretend it's not there.

Stop. Breathe. Ask it what it's trying to tell you.

And then - one small step at a time - move forward anyway.

That's how you use intuition when dealing with fear. Not by eliminating it. But by learning its language.

You've got this. You always have.

Ready to go deeper? Take the free assessment at intuitivesawaken.com/assessment to discover your intuitive type and get a personalized practice for working with fear.
